ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Epok-Epok Kentang; mapped to the closest parent class 'pastry food product' (FOODON_03302050) as a defensible approximate anchor. A more specific term does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Pastry, filled, fried) is the closest defensible match for a deep-fried filled pastry. No specific code for Epok-Epok or Malay curry puff exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No applicable SNOMED CT food concept identified for this traditional Malay fried pastry snack.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Epok-Epok Kentang is one piece weighing approximately 40 grams, commonly consumed as a breakfast item or teatime snack.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Selected Malaysian and Singaporean Foods — HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database for Deep-Fried Malay Snacks / IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore / Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Epok-Epok Kentang by name. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore nutrient composition data for comparable deep-fried filled pastry snacks (curry puff / karipap) and cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for similar fried dough-and-potato preparations. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 35% of total fat based on palm oil frying medium typical for this snack category, consistent with HPB deep-fried snack profiles. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat fractions set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown is not reported in the adapted source for this category. Cholesterol set to -1 as not reported. Water content set to -1 as not reported for this preparation.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values — Atkinson et al. 2008 / University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for wheat-flour fried pastry with potato filling
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Epok-Epok Kentang exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) deep-fried wheat-flour pastry shells with starchy fillings (curry puff / samosa analogues) reported in the University of Sydney GI Database with GI values in the range 60–75; (2) the moderating effect of fat from deep-frying, which slightly reduces the glucose response relative to baked wheat products. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (70 × 14.6) / 100 = 10.2. Carbohydrate per 40 g serving = 36.5 × 0.40 = 14.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg per 100 g; 152 mg per 40 g serving) combined with significant total fat from deep-frying
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Epok-Epok Kentang to occasional consumption only, as the sodium load of approximately 152 mg per piece contributes meaningfully toward the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg, and the saturated fat content from palm-oil frying further elevates cardiovascular risk associated with elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (estimated 70) and refined wheat-flour carbohydrate content (36.5 g per 100 g) causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Epok-Epok Kentang because the combination of refined wheat-flour pastry and starchy potato filling produces a high glycemic load (GL ≈ 10 per piece), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose spikes that are difficult to manage without medication adjustment.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation contributing elevated total fat (16.5 g per 100 g) and estimated saturated fat (5.8 g per 100 g), with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Epok-Epok Kentang as an occasional indulgence only, as the deep-fried pastry delivers approximately 6.6 g of total fat and 2.3 g of saturated fat per 40 g piece, alongside 152 mg sodium, all of which contribute to atherogenic risk and blood pressure elevation when consumed regularly.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory frying oils and nightshade ingredient (potato) may exacerbate symptoms in sensitive individuals, despite anti-inflammatory potential of curry spices
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume Epok-Epok Kentang cautiously, as the anti-inflammatory benefit of curry spices in the filling may be offset by the pro-inflammatory omega-6 fatty acids from deep-frying oil and the nightshade content of potato, which some patients report as a symptom trigger.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Epok-Epok Kentang is a traditional Malay deep-fried pastry snack popular across Malaysia and Singapore, where it is also known in Chinese as 咖喱角（马铃薯）, in Tamil as உருளைக்கிழங்கு கறி பஃப், and in Hindi as आलू करी पफ. It is closely related to, but distinct from, the larger curry puff (karipap): Epok-Epok features a notably thinner, crispier wheat-flour pastry shell that is hand-pleated into a crescent shape before being deep-fried in hot oil, typically palm oil, until golden brown. The filling consists of small cubes of potato sautéed with curry spices (turmeric, coriander, cumin), onions, and often tiny pieces of dried shrimp (udang kering) that add a savory umami depth. The result is a snack with a satisfying crunch on the outside and a warmly spiced, slightly starchy interior. Epok-Epok Kentang is a staple of Malay breakfast culture and afternoon tea (minum petang), sold at roadside gorengan (fried food) carts, pasar pagi (morning markets), and specialist kuih stalls. It is commonly paired with a spicy-sweet chili dipping sauce. Nutritionally, each 40 g piece provides approximately 126 kcal, 2.1 g protein, 6.6 g fat, and 14.6 g carbohydrate. The snack is energy-dense due to deep-frying and carries a high glycemic index (estimated 70), making it a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Sodium content is moderate-to-high at 380 mg per 100 g. The curry spices contribute minor amounts of iron, vitamin B6, and antioxidant compounds. Vitamin B12 and vitamin D are effectively absent in this plant-and-seafood-based preparation. The snack is halal-certified in most commercial preparations and is dairy-free, though it contains gluten from wheat flour and crustacean allergens from dried shrimp.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a curry puff?
What is the difference between epok-epok and karipap?
Is curry puff good for diabetics?
Can I eat curry puff if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Epok-Epok Kentang was found by name in a single approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore and IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for comparable deep-fried filled pastry snacks (curry puff / karipap category). The GI value is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using similar fried wheat-flour pastry products. Saturated fat is estimated from typical palm-oil frying profiles. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is set to 0.0: this is a plant-and-dried-seafood-based snack with no significant animal-derived B12 source; confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Singapore nutrient profiles for comparable fried pastry snacks. vitamin_d_mcg is set to 0.0: deep-fried wheat-flour and potato snack with no vitamin D-contributing ingredients; confirmed negligible per HPB Singapore data for this snack category. These are confirmed zero values, not missing data. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, cholesterol, and moisture data for this traditional preparation are not individually reported in the HPB Singapore or IMR Malaysia MyFCD databases for the curry puff / epok-epok category as of the accessed date. 3. Saturated fat (5.8 g per 100 g) is estimated at approximately 35% of total fat, consistent with palm oil as the typical frying medium for this snack category in Malaysia and Singapore, as documented in HPB Singapore deep-fried snack profiles. 4. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using fried wheat-flour pastry with starchy filling (samosa/curry puff analogues); no direct measurement for Epok-Epok Kentang exists in the database. GL per 40 g serving = (70 × 14.6) / 100 = 10.2. 5. This dish is popular in both Malaysia and Singapore; the Singaporean multilingual matrix has been applied to ensure Chinese (咖喱角（马铃薯）/ 咖喱角（馬鈴薯）), Tamil (உருளைக்கிழங்கு கறி பஃப்), and Hindi (आलू करी पफ) local names are present alongside the Malay name. Indonesian name (Pastel Kentang) is included as a regional variant. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology term for Epok-Epok Kentang exists in current public releases of FoodOn or FoodEx2. 7. Aliases consistency self-check: all strings in aliases_by_language verified to appear verbatim in flat aliases array — PASSED.

Iteration 3: Added Hindi local name 'आलू करी पफ' to local_names and aliases_by_language.hindi to satisfy G6 Hindi requirement.
Iteration 3: Added all new local-language strings verbatim to flat aliases array to satisfy Q8 aliases consistency rule.
Iteration 3: Added '咖喱角' (short-form Chinese alias) to aliases_by_language.chinese and flat aliases array for retrieval coverage.
Iteration 3: Applied Singaporean multilingual matrix (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) given the dish's strong Singaporean cultural presence alongside Malaysian origin.

Iteration 3: Updated notes to document multilingual matrix decision and aliases self-check result.
